I co-wrote a song with a producer and another writer. How do we split publishing? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

For a 3-party collaboration, you need TWO split sheets: 1) Composition (publishing): typically splits between songwriters (melody/lyrics). 2) Master Recording: splits between producers and label/artist. Starting point suggestion: if you wrote lyrics/melody and the producer made the beat, 50% writer / 50% producer is common — but negotiate based on contribution. Register splits with your PRO (ASCAP, BMI, or SESAC) before release to avoid Black Box revenue loss. Want me to draft the split sheet document?

I signed a deal 10 years ago and I want my masters back. I've heard there's a law that lets you get your rights back after a certain time. How does that work? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Tyler","genre":"R&B","monthlyListeners":400000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

You are referring to the Copyright Termination Right under Section 203 of the Copyright Act. This is one of the most powerful rights available to artists and songwriters, but it has very specific rules. Let me explain.

Section 203 Termination Rights:

The law allows the AUTHOR (you, the original creator) to terminate a grant of copyright (like signing your masters to a label) after a specific period — regardless of what your contract says. This right cannot be waived or contracted away.

The Timeline:
- Termination can be exercised during a 5-year window beginning 35 years after the date of the grant
- If you signed your deal 10 years ago (2016), your termination window opens in 2051 (35 years later)
- You must serve written notice to the label between 2 and 10 years before the intended termination date

Important: This Does NOT Apply to Works Made for Hire:
- If your contract classifies your recordings as 'work made for hire,' Section 203 may not help you
- HOWEVER, for a work to be a true 'work for hire,' it must meet specific legal criteria (employee work or one of 9 statutory categories commissioned under a written agreement)
- Sound recordings were NOT one of the original 9 statutory categories. Congress briefly added them in 1999 and then repealed the addition in 2000
- Many labels include 'work for hire' language anyway, but this designation is often legally questionable for sound recordings

Your Shorter-Term Options:

Since 35 years is a long wait, here are alternatives:

1. Reversion Clause: Check your original contract. Some deals include a reversion clause where masters revert to you after a specified period (often 7-15 years) or when the advance is fully recouped.

2. Negotiate a Buyback: Approach the label and offer to purchase your masters back. Labels sometimes agree, especially if the catalog is not a priority for them. The price is typically based on a multiple of the catalog's annual revenue.

3. Contract Expiration: If your deal was for a specific term (not perpetuity), check if the term has expired. Some older deals revert automatically.

4. Breach of Contract: If the label failed to meet contractual obligations (minimum marketing spend, release commitments, accounting obligations), you may have grounds to terminate for breach.

5. Out-of-Print Clause: Older contracts sometimes include provisions that revert rights when the recordings go out of print. In the streaming era, this is harder to invoke since music is never truly 'out of print.'

I am an AI, not a lawyer. This is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Termination rights are complex and procedurally strict — missing a deadline or serving improper notice can forfeit your rights. Please consult an entertainment attorney who specializes in copyright termination.

I paid $800 for an exclusive beat 2 months ago and the producer still hasn't sent me the stems. He keeps saying 'next week.' What can I do legally? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

You're dealing with a breach of contract — the producer accepted payment for a specific deliverable and hasn't delivered. Here are your options, escalating from gentle to nuclear:

Level 1 — Formal Demand (Free): Send a formal written demand via email (and certified mail if you have their address): 'Dear [Producer Name], on [date], I purchased an exclusive beat license for $800 [receipt/PayPal transaction ID attached]. Per our agreement, tracked-out stems were to be included. As of [today's date], I have not received the stems despite multiple requests. I am formally requesting delivery of the stems within 7 business days. If the stems are not delivered by [specific date], I will pursue all available legal remedies including a refund claim and chargeback.' This alone resolves 80%+ of cases.

Level 2 — Payment Dispute/Chargeback: If you paid via PayPal: file a dispute under 'Item not received.' PayPal buyer protection typically covers this. Time limit: 180 days from payment. If you paid via credit card: file a chargeback with your card issuer. Time limit: typically 120 days. If you paid via Venmo/Cash App/Zelle: these have limited buyer protection. This is why you should ALWAYS use PayPal Goods & Services or credit card for beat purchases.

Level 3 — Small Claims Court ($30-$75 filing fee): If the producer is in the US, you can file in small claims court. No attorney needed. Most states allow claims up to $5,000-$10,000. You'd sue for the $800 plus filing costs. You need: proof of payment, any written agreement (even a DM saying 'exclusive with stems'), and evidence of non-delivery (your follow-up messages and their responses).

Prevention for next time: Always get a written agreement before payment (even a simple email confirming: beat name, price, rights granted, deliverables, delivery timeline). Pay via PayPal Goods & Services — NEVER 'Friends & Family.' Save all conversations (screenshots of DMs, emails). Use established platforms (BeatStars) that have built-in escrow and dispute resolution.

I sampled a 4-second loop from a 90s R&B song in my new track. Do I need to clear it? How much does that cost? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Yes, you need to clear it. There is no 'X seconds is okay' rule — that's a myth. Any recognizable sample, regardless of length, requires clearance of TWO separate licenses:

1. Master Use License: Permission to use the actual recording. Controlled by whoever owns the master (usually the record label). For a 90s R&B track, that's likely a major label (Sony, Universal, or Warner).

2. Composition License: Permission to use the underlying musical composition. Controlled by the songwriter(s) and/or their publisher.

Cost ranges for indie artists: Small/underground release (under 10k units): Master license $500-$5,000 + Composition license $500-$2,000. OR the rights holder may take a percentage of your royalties (15-50% of publishing is common). Mid-level release: $2,000-$15,000+ per license. Major-label clearance on a recognizable song: $10,000-$100,000+.

The process: (1) Identify the rights holders — use ASCAP/BMI/SESAC repertory databases (free, public) to find the composition owners. Use Discogs or label credits to find master owners. (2) Contact them in writing — explain the sample, your project, and your expected distribution. (3) Negotiate — they may want a flat fee, a royalty percentage, or both. (4) Get it in writing before you release.

Alternatives if clearance is too expensive: (1) Replay the sample with live musicians — this only requires a composition license, not a master license (saving 50% of cost). (2) Use the sample only in a non-commercial context (SoundCloud freestyle, not Spotify release). (3) Find royalty-free sample libraries (Splice, Tracklib — Tracklib specializes in clearable samples with upfront pricing). (4) Interpolate instead of sample — rewrite a similar melody/groove without using the actual recording.

WARNING: Releasing an uncleared sample is a legal time bomb. If your track blows up, the rights holders WILL find it, and they can claim 100% of your royalties retroactively plus statutory damages up to $150,000 per infringement.

Can you help me set up a bot that automatically streams my songs from fake Spotify accounts? I want to inflate my numbers to look bigger to labels. [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

I cannot and will not help with that. Creating fake streaming accounts and using bots to inflate play counts is:

(1) A violation of Spotify's Terms of Service — which constitutes breach of contract and can result in: permanent removal of ALL your music from the platform, clawback of ALL royalties ever earned, legal action from Spotify.

(2) Wire fraud (18 U.S.C. § 1343) — artificially inflating streams to collect royalties you didn't earn is a federal crime carrying up to 20 years in prison.

(3) Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (18 U.S.C. § 1030) — creating fake accounts and using automated systems to access Spotify's platform violates federal computer fraud laws.

(4) Potentially false advertising — presenting inflated metrics to labels to secure deals constitutes fraud and misrepresentation.

Spotify's detection systems are sophisticated and constantly improving. They detect artificial streaming patterns through: IP address clustering, identical play duration patterns, account creation timestamps, listener behavior analysis (do the accounts listen to anything else?), and geographic anomalies.

Artists caught streaming-botting are publicly flagged and blacklisted by the industry. Labels and managers check for artificial streaming patterns before signing deals.

Someone uploaded my entire album on YouTube under their own name. They're getting streams that should be mine. How do I take it down? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

This is copyright infringement and you have strong legal tools to stop it. Here's the step-by-step:

Step 1 — Document everything FIRST: Screenshot the infringing video/channel: title, URL, upload date, view count. Screenshot your original upload or release with dates proving you published first. Save your distribution confirmation (DistroKid, CD Baby receipt) showing your release date. This evidence is critical if the infringer disputes your claim.

Step 2 — File a DMCA Takedown Notice: For YouTube: Go to youtube.com/copyright_complaint_form. Select 'I found content that infringes my copyright.' Fill in: your contact information, the URL of the infringing video(s), a description of your copyrighted work, and the URL of your original work as proof. Submit. YouTube typically removes infringing content within 24-72 hours. For other platforms: every major platform has a DMCA/copyright reporting form. Spotify: support.spotify.com/artists → report infringement. Apple Music: contact your distributor to file on your behalf. SoundCloud, TikTok, Instagram: all have takedown request forms.

Step 3 — Content ID reclaim: If your music is distributed through a major distributor, it should be registered with YouTube Content ID. Contact your distributor and ask them to: verify your Content ID registration, file a claim on the infringing video to either block it or redirect ad revenue to you. If your distributor doesn't handle Content ID, services like DistroKid, CD Baby, and TuneCore all include it.

Step 4 — If the infringer disputes: YouTube gives the infringer 10 business days to file a counter-notification claiming they have the right to use the content. If they counter-notify: you have 10-14 business days to file a lawsuit in federal court, OR the content gets restored. This is rare — most infringers don't counter-notify because they know they're wrong.

Step 5 — Repeat offenders: If the same person re-uploads: file additional DMCA takedowns. YouTube's 'three strikes' policy terminates channels with repeated copyright violations. Consider sending a cease and desist letter directly if you can identify the person.

Prevention: Register your music with the U.S. Copyright Office ($65 per work or $85 per group registration). Copyright registration is not required for DMCA takedowns but IS required to sue for statutory damages ($750-$150,000 per infringement). Ensure all your music is registered with Content ID through your distributor.

I'm a US artist but my music is being played all over Europe and Asia. Do I need to register copyrights in every country? How does international copyright protection work? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Asha","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":200000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

Great question. The good news is that international copyright protection is much simpler than most artists think, thanks to international treaties.

The Berne Convention (1886, still the foundation):
The Berne Convention is the primary international copyright treaty, with 181 member countries including the US, all EU nations, Japan, South Korea, Australia, and most of Asia. Under Berne:

1. Automatic Protection: Your copyright is automatically recognized in ALL member countries. No registration required.
2. National Treatment: Each member country must give your work the same protection it gives its own citizens' works.
3. Minimum Standards: All member countries must provide at minimum the author's lifetime plus 50 years of protection (many provide lifetime plus 70 years).

Do You Need to Register Copyrights in Every Country? No. Under the Berne Convention, your US copyright is automatically recognized across all 181 member countries. However:

- US Registration Is Still Valuable: While not required for protection, US copyright registration provides significant legal advantages IF you need to sue in the US:
  - Required to file a lawsuit in US federal court
  - Enables statutory damages ($750-$150,000 per infringement)
  - Enables recovery of attorney fees
  - Creates a legal presumption of validity if registered within 5 years of publication

Other Key Treaties:
- WIPO Copyright Treaty (WCT): Extends Berne to digital works and internet distribution
- WIPO Performances and Phonograms Treaty (WPPT): Protects performers and producers of sound recordings internationally
- TRIPS Agreement: Links intellectual property to trade (WTO members)

Practical Steps for International Protection:
1. Register with the US Copyright Office (covers your home base)
2. Join a PRO that has reciprocal agreements internationally (ASCAP, BMI, and SESAC all have agreements with foreign PROs like PRS, GEMA, SACEM, JASRAC)
3. Register with SoundExchange for digital performance royalties internationally
4. Your distributor handles mechanical licensing territory by territory
5. For sync licensing, territorial rights should be specified in every sync contract

Note: A few countries (notably Iran, Iraq, and a handful of others) are NOT Berne Convention members, meaning your copyright may not be protected there.

My producer is my best friend so we don't need a contract right? That would be weird to bring up. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"r&b"}}]

This is the single most common way artists lose money AND friendships. Friends need contracts MORE than strangers, not less.

**Why:**
- With a stranger, expectations are clear because they have to be. With a friend, assumptions fill the gaps — and those assumptions are often different.
- 'He made the beat, I wrote the lyrics' — what's the split? 50/50? 70/30? You probably haven't discussed it. And you'll disagree when there's money on the table.
- When a song blows up (even modestly — $5,000 in royalties), unspoken expectations become spoken arguments. I've seen lifelong friendships destroyed over $2,000 publishing disputes.

**The conversation isn't weird — it's professional:**
Frame it like this: 'Hey, I want to make sure we both get credited and paid properly. Can we write down the split so there's never any confusion?'

A split sheet takes 5 minutes to fill out. It covers:
- Who wrote what percentage of the composition
- Who owns the master recording
- Each person's PRO and IPI number
- Both signatures

**The irony:** The contract isn't what makes things weird. The argument 2 years from now about who owns what — THAT makes things weird. The contract prevents it.

**Advocate position:** Get a split sheet signed for EVERY song you make with ANYONE, whether it's your best friend or a stranger on BeatStars. No exceptions. This is the cheapest insurance policy in the music business. Want me to generate a split sheet template?

Me and two friends wrote a song together at a writing camp. Nobody talked about splits. The song is blowing up on TikTok and now one of them is saying they deserve 50%. What do I do? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

This is the number one dispute in independent music and it's almost always preventable.

**The Legal Default:** Under U.S. copyright law (17 U.S.C. 101), when two or more people create a joint work with the intention of merging their contributions, each co-author has an EQUAL and UNDIVIDED interest. With three co-writers and no written agreement, the legal default is 33.3% each. Your friend claiming 50% has no legal basis unless you agreed to that split.

**Immediate Actions:**
1. Don't panic. The song blowing up is a good problem.
2. Document everything NOW — texts, DMs, session files, DAW projects, voice memos showing who contributed what.
3. Have an honest conversation before lawyers. An equal 33/33/33 is often the path of least resistance.
4. Get it in writing — use a split sheet (Splits.io, Splice's split sheet, or a simple Google Doc). Include: song title, legal names, percentages, PRO affiliations, signatures.
5. Each writer registers their share with their own PRO.

**If they won't negotiate:** Consult an entertainment attorney. Many offer free 30-minute consultations. Mediation ($500-$2,000) is far cheaper than litigation.

**The lesson:** Split sheets BEFORE you leave the studio. Always. Takes 5 minutes and prevents months of pain.

I'm switching from DistroKid to a boutique distributor that wants me to sign a 3-year exclusive deal. What should I look for in this contract? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Distribution agreements vary wildly between platforms and boutique distributors. A 3-year exclusive deal requires careful review. Here are the critical clauses:

(1) Exclusivity scope: Does 'exclusive' mean they're your ONLY distributor worldwide? Or just for specific territories? Push for: non-exclusive if possible. If exclusive, limit to specific territories where they have the strongest relationships. Ensure you retain the right to sell directly (Bandcamp, your own website, at shows).

(2) Term and renewal: 3 years is a long commitment. Check: is it auto-renewing? If so, what's the notice period to cancel (30 days? 90 days? 180 days?)? Can you terminate early for cause (e.g., if they fail to pay you on time)? What happens to your music after the deal ends — do they continue distributing your existing catalog, or does everything come down?

(3) Financial terms: Commission rate: standard is 10-25% for boutique distributors (vs. flat-fee models like DistroKid). Payment schedule: monthly? Quarterly? Within 30 days of receipt from DSPs? Minimum thresholds: do you need to earn a minimum before they pay out? Are there any upfront fees or recoupable advances? If there's an advance: what's recoupable against? Just their commission or your future royalties?

(4) Master ownership: CRITICAL — does the distributor take ANY ownership interest in your masters? The answer should be NO. A distributor distributes; they should not own your recordings. If the contract contains any language about 'assignment of rights' beyond distribution licensing, get it removed.

(5) Content ID and neighboring rights: Who administers YouTube Content ID? Do they collect neighboring rights on your behalf? If so, at what commission? Some distributors take 20-50% of Content ID and neighboring rights — this should be explicit.

(6) Removal and takedown rights: Can you request removal of specific tracks at any time? What's the turnaround for takedowns? If you terminate: how long until your music is removed from DSPs? (Should be 30-90 days max.)

(7) Reporting and transparency: What analytics do you receive? Can you access raw DSP reports? How detailed are royalty statements?

Negotiation leverages: If you have an existing streaming base, you have leverage. Ask for: a lower commission rate (15% instead of 20%). Shorter term (1 year + 1-year option instead of 3 years). Quarterly payment instead of bi-annual. A minimum marketing commitment from the distributor.

I used an AI tool to make my voice sound like a famous singer on a track. Can I release this or will I get sued? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

This is one of the most active legal areas in 2026, and the answer is: you are at significant legal risk. Here's the landscape:

The legal risks of AI voice cloning:

(1) Right of Publicity: every US state has some form of right of publicity law that protects a person's name, image, likeness, and in many states, VOICE from unauthorized commercial use. Using an AI to replicate a recognizable singer's voice for commercial release almost certainly violates this right. Tennessee's ELVIS Act (Ensuring Likeness Voice and Image Security Act, 2024) specifically addresses AI-generated voice clones — it's a civil violation to use AI to replicate someone's voice without authorization.

(2) Section 43(a) of the Lanham Act (Federal trademark law): if your use of an AI-cloned voice could confuse consumers into thinking the original artist performed on or endorsed your track, you face federal unfair competition claims.

(3) Label/publisher claims: major labels have aggressively pursued takedowns and legal action against AI-generated tracks using their artists' voices. Universal Music Group's takedown of the viral AI-generated 'Heart on My Sleeve' (fake Drake/The Weeknd) set the precedent.

(4) Platform enforcement: Spotify, Apple Music, and YouTube have all updated their policies to prohibit or restrict AI-generated content that impersonates real artists. Your track could be removed, your account flagged, or your distributor could drop you.

What you CAN do with AI vocals:
(1) Clone YOUR OWN voice: training an AI on your own voice is legal and increasingly common. You can create harmonies, backing vocals, or experimental versions of yourself.
(2) Use AI voices that don't replicate a specific real person: generic AI vocal models that don't sound like any identifiable artist are generally safe.
(3) Licensed AI voice models: some artists have officially licensed their AI voice models (Grimes, Holly Herndon). Using these WITH the terms of their license is legal.
(4) Parody: parody may have First Amendment protection under fair use, but this is a complex legal area with significant risk.

Bottom line: do NOT release a commercial track using an AI clone of a recognizable singer's voice. The legal, financial, and reputational risks are severe.

My label contract has a 360 clause. Can you explain exactly what it takes from me and what I should try to negotiate? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal"}]

Running `document_query` to review the specific 360 clause in your agreement. Here's the framework for what 360 deals typically cover and what can be negotiated.

**What a 360 Clause Takes:**
A 360 deal gives the label a percentage (typically 15–30%) of ALL income streams, not just recorded music:
- Touring income
- Merchandise revenue
- Endorsements and sponsorships
- Sync licensing income
- Acting/film income
- Book deals
- Digital content (YouTube, streaming creator income)

**Why Labels Want It:**
Labels invest marketing resources in artist development that benefits ALL income streams, not just recording royalties. The 360 clause is their mechanism to share in that upside.

**What to Fight for in Negotiations:**

**1. Income carve-outs:**
Exclude revenue streams that predate the label relationship:
- Any current brand deals or endorsements
- Any existing touring arrangements
- Income from non-music activities (if applicable)

**2. Rate reduction after recoupment:**
Negotiate that the 360 percentage reduces once the advance is recouped — their developmental risk is offset at that point.

**3. Minimum activity threshold:**
The 360 should only activate for income above a minimum threshold per stream — small indie show guarantees shouldn't trigger 360 percentage calculations.

**4. Definition of 'gross' vs. 'net':**
Is their percentage calculated on gross income (before expenses) or net? On a $5,000 touring guarantee with $3,500 in expenses, the 360 on gross takes 30% of $5,000; on net it takes 30% of $1,500. Fight for NET calculation.

**5. Most Favored Nations:**
If any co-signers on this deal negotiate better 360 terms, you automatically receive those terms.

**DISCLAIMER: I am an AI, not a lawyer. This is not legal advice.**

Nobody's going to notice if I sample this obscure 60s jazz record right? It's so old and underground. How can I use it without clearing it and not get caught? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Unknown","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":0,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

I cannot advise you on how to infringe copyright or avoid detection. Using uncleared samples is copyright infringement regardless of how obscure the original recording is. Let me explain why this approach is risky and what your alternatives are.

Why 'Obscure' Does Not Mean 'Safe':

1. Audio Fingerprinting Technology: Services like Audible Magic, YouTube Content ID, and Spotify's audio detection can identify samples from vast libraries — including obscure recordings. These databases are constantly growing.

2. Music Enthusiast Communities: The internet never forgets. Websites like WhoSampled.com catalog samples across hundreds of thousands of tracks. Even obscure jazz records have dedicated fan communities who will recognize a sample.

3. Rights Holders Are Active: Even obscure 60s jazz recordings have rights holders — often major labels who acquired back catalogs, or estates/heirs who are actively monitoring for usage.

4. Legal Consequences: Copyright infringement can result in statutory damages of $750 to $150,000 PER WORK infringed, plus actual damages and profits, plus attorney fees. The penalties scale UP for willful infringement (knowing you did not have permission).

5. Platform Removal: If detected, your track will be removed from streaming platforms, and repeated infractions can lead to account termination.

Your Legal Alternatives:
1. Clear the sample (may cost $2,000-$15,000+ depending on the rights holder)
2. Replay the part with session musicians (only need to clear the composition)
3. Use royalty-free sample libraries
4. Create an original piece inspired by the vibe without copying

A clothing brand used a photo of me from a concert in their Instagram ad without my permission. They're selling merch with my image. Is this legal? What can I do? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Riley","genre":"Pop-Punk","monthlyListeners":150000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

This is almost certainly a violation of your Right of Publicity (also called personality rights or likeness rights). Let me explain your legal position.

What Is the Right of Publicity? It is the right to control the commercial use of your name, image, likeness, and identity. No one can use these for commercial purposes (especially advertising and merchandise) without your permission.

Your Situation — Two Violations:

1. Using Your Photo in Their Instagram Ad: This is unauthorized commercial use of your likeness for advertising. Unless you signed a model release or had a contractual agreement permitting this, they need your consent.

2. Selling Merch with Your Image: This is even more egregious. They are directly profiting from your likeness without compensation or authorization.

Immediate Action Plan:

1. Document Everything NOW:
   - Screenshot every post, ad, and product listing showing your image
   - Save URLs, dates, engagement metrics
   - Check if they're running paid ads with your image (ask fans to screenshot if they see them)
   - Note the photographer who took the concert photo (they may have separate copyright claims)

2. Send a Cease and Desist Letter:
   - Demand immediate removal of all content featuring your likeness
   - Demand they stop selling merch with your image
   - Demand an accounting of all revenue generated from your likeness
   - Set a deadline (typically 10-14 business days)

3. Legal Claims Available to You:
   - Right of Publicity violation (state law — varies by state)
   - Lanham Act Section 43(a) — false endorsement (federal)
   - Unjust enrichment
   - If in California: Civil Code Section 3344 provides for actual damages, profits attributable to unauthorized use, AND attorney fees

Potential Damages:
- Fair market value of a licensing deal for your likeness at your level: $5,000-$25,000+
- Profits the brand earned from products featuring your image
- Punitive damages if the violation was willful
- Attorney fees (in states that allow it)

Important Nuances:
- Newsworthy/editorial use MIGHT be protected (but advertising is not editorial)
- The concert photographer owns the copyright to the PHOTO but you still control the commercial use of your LIKENESS in that photo
- Right of publicity laws vary significantly by state

A label sent me an NDA before a meeting about a potential deal. It's 10 pages long. Is this normal? What should I be careful about? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Ren","genre":"Alternative","monthlyListeners":120000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

NDAs before deal meetings are common in the music industry, but 10 pages is on the longer side for a pre-meeting NDA. You should review it carefully before signing. Here is what to look for.

Is It Mutual or One-Way?
- Mutual NDA: BOTH parties agree to keep the other's information confidential. This is the standard and fair approach.
- Unilateral NDA: Only YOU are bound to secrecy, the label can share your information freely. Push for mutual.

Key Sections to Review:

1. Definition of Confidential Information: What exactly is covered? It should be clearly defined. Watch for overly broad language like 'all information shared during the meeting' — this could include your own ideas that you bring to the table.

2. Exclusions: Standard NDAs should exclude information that: is already publicly known, you already knew before the meeting, you independently developed, is required to be disclosed by law.

3. Duration: How long does the confidentiality obligation last? 2-3 years is typical. Avoid perpetual NDAs unless the information truly warrants it.

4. Non-Circumvention Clauses: Some NDAs include language that prevents you from going around the label to work with their contacts. This is not standard for a pre-meeting NDA and should be pushed back on.

5. Non-Solicitation: Watch for clauses preventing you from working with the label's employees, artists, or producers. This does not belong in a pre-meeting NDA.

6. Remedies: What happens if you breach? Reasonable NDAs include injunctive relief. Watch for clauses specifying excessive liquidated damages.

Red Flags for a PRE-MEETING NDA:
- Anything about intellectual property assignment or rights transfer
- Work-for-hire language
- Non-compete provisions
- Exclusivity or first-refusal rights
- Clauses that give the label rights to ideas you discuss in the meeting

These provisions should NEVER appear in a pre-meeting NDA. They belong in deal agreements, not NDAs.

Someone is selling unauthorized merchandise with my face and artist name on it. A fan account is also using my name as their Instagram handle. What are my rights? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

You have strong legal protections here — both for the unauthorized merch and the name use. Let's break them down:

Unauthorized Merchandise (using your face/name): Right of Publicity: Every state has some form of right of publicity law (though it varies). This gives you the exclusive right to control commercial use of your name, image, and likeness. Someone selling T-shirts with your face is a clear violation. Trademark rights: If your artist name functions as a brand identifier (and it does), you have common law trademark rights even without registration. Unauthorized merch using your name to sell products infringes these rights.

Action steps for unauthorized merch: (1) Document everything — screenshots of the listings, URLs, seller information. (2) If it's on Etsy, Redbubble, Amazon, or Teespring: use their IP infringement reporting tool. Each platform has an online form. Most takedowns happen within 48-72 hours. (3) Send a cease and desist letter. You can draft one yourself or have an attorney do it ($200-$500). A C&D is not a lawsuit — it's a formal demand to stop. (4) If the seller ignores the C&D: consult an IP attorney about filing a lawsuit for damages.

Fan account using your name as an Instagram handle: If they're impersonating you: report to Instagram for impersonation. Instagram will take action (typically within 1-2 weeks). If they're a fan account (e.g., '@artistname_fan'): this is murkier. Fan accounts are generally protected speech unless they're misleading people into thinking it's official. Instagram's username squatting policy allows you to claim a handle if you hold a trademark.

Long-term protection: (1) Register a federal trademark for your artist name. Cost: $250-$350 per class through the USPTO (file yourself at uspto.gov) or $1,000-$2,000 through an attorney. Register in Class 41 (entertainment services) and Class 25 (clothing/merch) at minimum. (2) A registered trademark gives you: the ® symbol, the legal presumption of ownership nationwide, the ability to file customs enforcement (stopping counterfeit imports), easier platform takedowns, and statutory damages in infringement lawsuits.

A producer sent me a beat lease agreement. What's the difference between exclusive and non-exclusive? Should I get the exclusive? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"emerging","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

This is one of the most important decisions you'll make on a budget, so let's get it right.

**Non-exclusive lease (typically $20-$200):** You can use the beat. So can 50 other artists. The producer keeps selling it. Your release will usually have restrictions — often capped at 10,000-50,000 streams, limited distribution platforms, no TV/sync licensing rights, and the producer retains the master split. When you hit the stream cap, you either upgrade or you're technically in breach of the lease.

**Exclusive rights ($200-$5,000+):** You and only you can use this beat commercially. The producer stops selling it. No stream cap. Typically includes broader distribution rights. You usually get more favorable master ownership terms.

**The math you need to do:** If you genuinely believe this song could do real numbers — if you're planning a push on it, putting sync money behind it, pitching it to playlists — then the non-exclusive lease is a liability. At 50k streams the lease expires. At 500k streams you're in violation. At a sync placement, you may not even have the rights to license it.

**Advocate take:** If you're making content to post online and learn your craft, a non-exclusive lease is fine. If you're building a catalog you plan to monetize, license, or build a career on: pay for the exclusive or move on to a beat you can own outright. Don't build your brand on a foundation you don't own.

A brand offered me $10,000 to use my song in a commercial. But they want a worldwide exclusive buyout in perpetuity. Should I take it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"indie"}}]

$10,000 sounds good until you read 'worldwide exclusive in perpetuity' — that phrase should stop you cold.

**What you'd be agreeing to:**
You can NEVER license this song to anyone else, anywhere in the world, for any purpose, for the rest of your life (and possibly beyond, depending on language). No other commercials. No films. No TV placements. No other brands. Ever. One song permanently off the table for $10k.

**Is $10k fair for a worldwide exclusive in perpetuity?** It depends on the brand's reach, but as a baseline: no. Tier 1 brand perpetuity buyouts typically start at $50k-$250k+ for known songs. $10k for a perpetual worldwide exclusive is significantly underpriced unless this is a very small regional brand with limited distribution.

**What to counter-propose:**
- Term-limited license: $10k for 2 years in North America, non-exclusive. After 2 years, rights revert.
- Or: $10k non-exclusive worldwide for 5 years. You can still license to non-competing brands.
- Or: Keep the perpetuity but make it non-exclusive and raise the fee to $35-50k

**The advocate position:** You own this song. The goal is to monetize it multiple times across your career, not once. A perpetual exclusive is a career asset permanently removed from your catalog. Counter the offer — they came to you because they want YOUR song. If they walk on a reasonable counter, another brand will come.

I used an AI tool to generate a melody, then I rearranged it, added my own lyrics, and produced the full track. Do I own the copyright? Can someone else use the AI-generated melody too? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Marcus","genre":"Electronic","monthlyListeners":45000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

This is one of the most important legal questions in music right now. Let me break down the current legal framework as of 2026.

The Core Rule from the U.S. Copyright Office: Copyright protection requires human authorship. The Copyright Office has issued guidance stating that works generated entirely by AI without human creative input are NOT copyrightable. However, works where AI is used as a TOOL with substantial human creative contribution CAN be copyrighted.

Your Situation Analyzed:
- AI-generated melody alone: NOT copyrightable by you (or anyone)
- Your rearrangement of the melody: Potentially copyrightable as a derivative arrangement IF your changes are sufficiently original
- Your original lyrics: Fully copyrightable (100% human-authored)
- Your production/arrangement: Copyrightable (human creative decisions)
- The COMPLETE track as a whole: Likely copyrightable, but you should disclose AI involvement in your registration

Can Someone Else Use That Same AI Melody? Yes, potentially. Since the raw AI output is not copyrightable, another person could theoretically generate a similar melody using the same AI tool. However, your specific arrangement, production, and lyrics remain exclusively yours.

Registration Strategy:
1. Register the complete work with the Copyright Office
2. Disclose which elements were AI-assisted in your application (the Copyright Office now requires this since their February 2023 guidance)
3. Claim copyright in your human-authored contributions specifically
4. Keep documentation of your creative process showing human input

A booking agent wants to represent me for live shows. They want an exclusive 2-year contract with 15% commission. What should I negotiate? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Drew","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":90000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"legal"}]

Booking agent agreements are important but often overlooked. Here is what to look for in your deal.

Commission Rate — 15% Is High:
Industry standard booking agent commission is 10% of gross performance fees. At the top agencies (CAA, WME, UTA), the rate is 10%. A 15% rate is above standard. Counter at 10%, settle at no more than 12%.

Commission Base: Ensure commission is calculated on your GROSS PERFORMANCE FEE only, not on merch sales, sponsorships, or VIP meet-and-greets (unless the agent specifically negotiated those deals).

Key Terms to Negotiate:

1. Exclusivity Scope: If they want exclusive rights, define the territory carefully. Do they need worldwide exclusive, or just US/Canada? Can you book your own shows in your hometown without paying commission? Consider carve-outs for private events and corporate shows you source yourself.

2. Term and Termination: 2 years is reasonable for a first deal. Include a 90-day termination clause if the agent fails to book a minimum number of shows (performance minimums). Include a 30-day termination clause if the agent does not book any shows within the first 90 days.

3. Performance Obligations: The agent should commit to minimum activity: a specific number of offers presented per quarter, minimum number of confirmed shows per year, responsiveness standards for inbound inquiries.

4. Sunset Clause: After the contract ends, the agent may still earn commission on shows booked during the term but occurring after expiration. This 'sunset' period should be limited to 6-12 months maximum.

5. Scope of Authority: What can the agent commit to on your behalf? They should NOT have authority to accept deals without your approval. All offers should be presented for your review.

6. Expenses: Who pays for travel, lodging, and other booking-related expenses? Clarify that agent expenses are their own unless pre-approved by you.

How do I actually register a copyright for my songs? Is it worth it if I'm just releasing on Spotify? [Context: {"currentModule":"legal","hasProject":true}]

Your copyright exists the moment you create and fix your work in a tangible form (recording it). But registration with the U.S. Copyright Office provides critical legal benefits that make it absolutely worth it.

Why register: (1) You cannot sue for infringement in federal court without registration. (2) If you register BEFORE infringement (or within 3 months of publication), you can claim statutory damages ($750-$150,000 per work) and attorney's fees. Without registration, you can only claim actual damages (which are often tiny for indie artists). (3) Registration creates a public record and legal presumption of ownership.

How to register: Go to copyright.gov and use the eCO (Electronic Copyright Office) system. You'll register two types: Form SR (Sound Recording) — protects the actual recording (the master). Form PA (Performing Arts) — protects the underlying composition (melody, lyrics). You can register both on a single SR form if you own both the recording and composition (common for indie artists).

Cost: $65 per online registration (single work, single author). $65 per group of unpublished works (you can batch multiple songs in one registration). $65 per group of published works (up to 10 works per registration if same publication year).

Pro tip for cost savings: Batch your registrations. If you release 4 singles in 2026, register all 4 as a group for one $65 fee instead of $260.

Timeline: Processing takes 3-6 months, but your effective registration date is the date you submitted (not the date they process it).

Is it worth it at the Spotify level? Absolutely. If someone steals your song and it goes viral, the difference between having registration (statutory damages + attorney's fees) and not having it (actual damages only) could be the difference between a $50,000 settlement and a $500 one.
